data.title: Taxpayers foot bill to save £15m rail yard sunk by Reeves raid
data.description:
Labour is to spend taxpayers’ money on taking over a £15m rail-freight project abandoned by Eurotunnel after Rachel Reeves tripled the company’s business rates bill.

Network Rail is poised to step in to save a planned upgrade of the Barking Eurohub freight terminal in east London, with the project to be bankrolled with government money.

Eurotunnel walked away from the project and its other UK investments in November, when the Treasury revealed that its rates bill would jump from £22m to £65m.

The upgrade of the Barking Eurohub is viewed as vital to kickstarting cargo flows through the Channel Tunnel after only a handful of freight trains used the link last year.

The Department for Transport (DfT) said Network Rail, which is to be merged with nationalised train companies to form Great British Railways, would take control of the yard and its property development arm, Platform4. 

DfT described it as a “government-backed deal” for the Barking site, which is currently owned by Legal & General. 

Network Rail said it was in talks with potential future operators for the terminal, which could bring in £15m of investment, while “getting on with delivering the works required to reinvigorate the site and make it fit for purpose.”

A spokesman for Network Rail said the track and station operator would work to “transform the site into a cargo hub”.

Labour ‘driving away investment’

However, critics criticised the deal on Wednesday. Jerome Mayhew, the shadow rail minister, claimed that Network Rail was being forced to step in after the Chancellor’s “astronomical” business rates increase drove out Eurotunnel.

He said: “When business leaders and rail operators warned that these punitive increases would have real-world consequences, this is exactly what they meant.

“Labour’s own tax decisions have driven away investment and forced taxpayers to underwrite infrastructure that should have been delivered commercially.”

The DfT argued that the plan for the Barking terminal would revive the cross-Channel rail freight market and boost trade links with Europe, helping to stimulate the economy and reducing road congestion and emissions.

It said the key to the redevelopment would be to allow full-size European container trains restricted to the High Speed 1 route to reach London.

That would enable food markets to receive fruit and vegetables direct from overseas growers and open up the possibility of Scotch whisky and British consumer items being exported by rail.

Network Rail said it envisaged three trains a day running into the terminal from the Continent, connecting with services to Scotland via central England and the North West and removing 140,000 HGVs from the roads each year.

Maggie Simpson, the director general of the Rail Freight Group, said the Government’s intervention was necessary to “keep the dream of Channel Tunnel cargo alive” and safeguard a site whose loss would have prevented container trains from ever using the link.

However, she said that factors including unfair cost advantages that favour the trucking industry would need to be addressed if significant volumes of freight were to use the tunnel.

Ms Simpson said ministers were also compelled to step in because of the Barking yard’s importance to containerised defence-related shipments at a time of heightening international tension.

She said: “If you look at Britain’s strategic resilience it is a vital national asset for whatever you want to transport to and from Europe, and that includes defence shipments”.

The Barking site, originally developed by Deutsche Bahn’s DB Cargo for automotive shipments, received the first direct freight train from China to the UK in 2017, when a rake of 34 containers arrived there after an 18-day journey.

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: 100m euros, a giant drill and two days undetected: Inside Europe’s biggest bank job
data.description:
The first fire alarm at a branch of the Sparkasse savings bank in the heart of the Buer district of Gelsenkirchen, Germany, sounded just after 6am on Saturday, December 27 last year. Firefighters were dispatched, along with officers from the private security firm contracted by the bank. They found nothing – no smoke, no smell of burning, no visible damage. The rolling shutter of the basement vault room was intact. They logged a false alarm and left.

Even as the firefighters inspected the scene, behind that shutter, a criminal gang of between five and seven men was at work, pulling off what is now thought to be the biggest bank heist in European history. The haul was originally estimated to be in the region of €30m (£26m) in cash and valuables, but some reports place it closer to €100m. The real amount may never be established.

It was not until two days later – at 3.58am on Monday, December 29 – that a second fire alarm summoned the emergency services back to the unstaffed Sparkasse. What police and firefighters discovered this time was nothing short of cinematic. The reinforced concrete wall of an archive room adjacent to the main vault gaped with a drilled hole 40cm in diameter. As the voluble Gelsenkirchen police spokesman Thomas Nowaczyk tells me when I visit the force’s bustling major incident room 10 weeks after the robbery, it was “about the size of a washing machine’s porthole”.

Through this aperture lay a scene of chaos. The floor of the underground vault room was carpeted with the discarded contents of more than 3,000 safe deposit boxes, each cracked open with brute force; papers, envelopes, velvet pouches, family photographs, the accumulated intimate matter of thousands of private lives, all now festered in centimetres of water, which the perpetrators had redirected from the ladies’ toilet to cool their drill.

Herbert Reul, the interior minister for the state of North Rhine-Westphalia, later described the aftermath as resembling “eine Müllkippe” – a rubbish tip. Apparently cast aside by the robbers, along with items of no obvious financial value, was anything likely to bear an identifying serial number: Rolex watches, for example. “A great deal of jewellery and expensive timepieces were left behind,” confirms Nowaczyk. “Gold and cash – that is what they wanted.”

And that is what they got. Having potentially been on site for “several days”, according to Nowaczyk, the gang had already left the scene by the time the police arrived. But not before one of their number, dressed in a grey jumper and a balaclava, was filmed on the car park’s CCTV dutifully paying their parking fee. Reul confirmed that police have been evaluating eight terabytes of data from inside the bank and the surrounding area. What, if anything, it shows of the perpetrators at work is yet to be disclosed.

To understand how the thieves got in – and then got away – over those two dramatic days in December, it is helpful to picture the neighbourhood surrounding the bank. Facing the centuries-old Buer marketplace, the branch of Sparkasse on Nienhofstrasse occupies the lower three floors of an apartment building. Above it shines the bank’s illuminated “S” insignia. To its rear is the publicly accessible Marientor multi-storey car park, whose lowest level connects, via a shuttered ramp, to the basement car park of the Sparkasse building.

Reserved for the landlord and a few others with special access, this private parking facility has a staircase leading to the basement floor of the bank, and a fire door that, under normal circumstances, opens only from the inside. “We don’t know yet whether it was forced open, or if it had already been tampered with from within,” Nowaczyk tells me. Certainly, at the time of the robbery, the motion sensor behind the door had been taped over.

Germany’s extended Christmas holiday – shops, banks and most businesses close from the evening of December 24 until at least December 27, with many remaining shut until the new year – gave the thieves their opportunity. From Christmas Eve, the streets of Buer were quiet, and the Marientor car park largely empty. As Femi Altas, whose clothing alterations service overlooks the bank, puts it: “They chose their moment perfectly.”

The bank’s computer data, later scrutinised by investigators, show the first safe deposit box being breached at 10.45am on December 27, and the last at 2.44pm. Witnesses report seeing several men carrying large bags in the staircase of the garage overnight on Saturday into Sunday. Security camera footage recovered from the car park records a black Audi RS 6 leaving in the early hours of Monday morning with masked occupants inside. The vehicle’s number plate had been stolen earlier in Hanover, a city more than 140 miles north-east of Gelsenkirchen. A second vehicle, a white Mercedes Citan van, was also identified in the footage; it too had false plates.

Neither vehicle has been found so far. “Of course, we are now looking nationwide for these vehicles, and evaluating many terabytes of camera footage,” says Nowaczyk. “A huge task, even with the help of AI.”

On the morning of December 29, the residents of Gelsenkirchen woke to reports of the robbery. By midday, the news had gone national. By afternoon, hundreds of dismayed Sparkasse customers thronged outside the branch, some of them chanting, “Let us in”, and pushing towards the entrance. The bank remained closed, with the police citing threats made against its employees. The crowd were not wealthy individuals whose investment portfolios had taken a hit, à la Bernie Madoff. One man told the TV channel Welt that he could not sleep for not knowing what had become of his quarter-of-a-century’s worth of savings.

These were careworn people for whom the contents of a small steel box represented everything they had set aside, sometimes over decades, for old age, for emergencies or for the benefit of their children. Femi Altas was not directly affected, he says, although many of his customers and friends were; and he has noticed that, since the raid, trade across the neighbourhood has dipped. What’s more, he tells me, multiple generations of history filled those boxes. “Not just money but a family’s story – from Turkey, from Germany, from everywhere. Gone.”

A tearful Nezahat Kilinc, a client of the bank since 1990, told the German public-service broadcaster ZDF how she had lost cherished rings in the robbery, items that had belonged to her brother, who died aged 25 in a traffic accident.

As Nowaczyk tells me, the scale of the crime has been “a punch in the stomach” for the community. A predominantly working-class city in the far west of Germany, Gelsenkirchen once powered the national industrial revolution with steel and coal. After both industries collapsed in the 1980s and Nineties, it never fully recovered and, with a population of roughly 260,000, it remains one of the poorest places in Germany per capita. During the Euro 2024 football championship held in the country, an England fan named Paul Brown went viral after declaring the city an “absolute s---hole” on X – although Nowaczyk points out that it is “good enough for Taylor Swift”, who played the city’s Veltins-Arena for three sold-out nights the same year.

The Buer district is one of the older, more respectable parts of the city; a place of solid, Wilhelminian apartment blocks, family-run bakeries, and a savings bank branch that has served the community for generations. The Sparkasse – Germany’s network of publicly owned regional savings banks – occupies a place of trust. It is something between a utility and a community institution, the financial equivalent of the local post office. Arguably, the Sparkasse is a riposte to the German playwright Bertolt Brecht’s much-quoted provocation “What is the robbing of a bank compared to the founding of a bank?” Its customers are tradespeople, nurses, former miners and retired factory workers.

Nevertheless, safe deposit boxes can contain substantial sums. Lawyer Daniel Kuhlmann told a press conference in January that he is representing a victim who had lost €400,000 in cash in the robbery, after depositing it in a box. That customer is one of a cohort of Germans who have saved carefully for decades and who, more than almost any other demographic in Europe, prefer to do so using tangible assets. 

Germany’s relationship with wealth is complicated. The hyperinflation of the 1920s and the post-Second World War currency reform, when savings were wiped out overnight, cast long shadows. Germans maintain relatively large currency reserves at home – an average of €1,364 per person, according to a 2018 study – and they rank among the highest in Europe for their cash payment preferences: in 2023, 51 per cent of all transactions were still settled in hard currency.

Hence the popularity of the safe deposit box, or schliessfach, particularly among those with an acute distrust of the formal financial system – including many in Germany’s substantial Turkish and Middle Eastern diaspora communities.

A significant proportion of the population of Gelsenkirchen – 37.6 per cent, according to the regional statistics board – has a “migration background”, many with roots in Turkey or other parts of the Middle East, where family wealth has historically been held in gold. Cash and bullion can make dealing with inheritance across jurisdictions simpler, and less subject to scrutiny – and tax. These factors help explain the surprisingly valuable contents of those ransacked safe deposit boxes.

For a modest annual fee – typically €60-150 – a customer rents a small steel box in the savings bank’s vault, the contents of which are entirely their own business. The bank does not know – and legally mustn’t know – what is inside, although it holds the master key. The customer receives their own key, and can visit the vault at will during business hours. Although some savings banks have upgraded to automated retrievals and private cabins, typically a corner of the vault room is given over to a table where customers can inspect their belongings. This is the setup at the Buer branch of Sparkasse.

Available in various dimensions, each box at the branch was insured by the bank for a maximum of €10,300 – a figure enshrined in German banking conventions. The total insured value of all 3,250 boxes is therefore around €33.5m – hence the figure initially reported as the size of the Gelsenkirchen robbery. Some victims have told police that the contents of their boxes far exceeded the insured value, meaning the true figure could dwarf this estimate.

That the Gelsenkirchen break-in was perpetrated by “insiders” has been a common refrain in German media reports. Nowaczyk points out: “In the crowded vault room, the hole was drilled in precisely the right place. That required prior knowledge.” Accordingly, all current and former Sparkasse employees have been interviewed by police, as well as those working for the (unnamed) private security firm.

“But it could also be someone who had simply rented a schliessfach,” notes Nowaczyk. At first, the appeal for information from box-holders was made on a voluntary basis, resulting in more than 1,500 interviews by February 12. Since then, the public prosecutor has been handling summonses, says Nowaczyk. “We have to talk to everyone, and it will take some time.”

On February 19, a packed public meeting was held in the Schauburg Filmpalast cinema, a three-minute walk from the Sparkasse, moderated by local radio presenters and broadcast online. Fellow victims Petra and Sigrid sat together, among the successful few out of thousands hoping to squeeze into the 420-capacity auditorium. I listened on my laptop as Petra spoke quietly to the gathering. “I had jewellery and money in a safe deposit box – savings of many decades. It was the savings I’d put aside for retirement, which were supposed to make life a little easier, and now it’s all gone. And that haunts me every day.”

Sigrid had not yet absorbed the shock. “There’s still anger and bewilderment and sleepless nights,” she said. “You think about it all the time, and you’re constantly reminded of it – and yes, you’re also angry that you now have to prove that you had something in the safe deposit box at all.”

Victims have been asked to provide as much evidence as possible of what they stored – photographs, insurance certificates, receipts, anything that might help identify or verify the content. For objects accumulated over lifetimes, often inherited, often never formally recorded, this is patently unrealistic. One audience member named Jochen, a Sparkasse customer since childhood, stood up to vent his frustration. He described being informed years ago that he could increase the insurance coverage on his box, then being told the scheme was no longer available, and now being unable to prove that either conversation happened. “I trusted them,” he said.

Ingo Aulbach of the Association of Insurance Brokers notes that private insurance for safe deposit box contents is readily available – policies through household insurance or specialist safe and valuables cover can extend far beyond the €10,300 bank cap, and can even cover high-value cash holdings. Still, he acknowledges, these precautions require a level of disclosure that many customers are reluctant to make.

A taxi driver called Hüseyin tells me that he has a close friend in just such a predicament. As we drive past a vast, grassed-over slag heap – one of dozens that still punctuate the Ruhr skyline, and a reminder of Hüseyin’s late father, who worked as a coal miner – Hüseyin says of his friend: “He stored 20-30kg of gold at the Sparkasse. [He] brought it over from Turkey on a flight, which I told him was insane. Anyway, although he has the paperwork, he can’t report it to the police because he hadn’t paid the import duty. So it’s a write-off. A lot of what was in those boxes, I think, came from illegal business or the grey economy.”

Unsurprisingly, the bank has had to field criticism of its security measures. “The Sparkasse, like the customer, is a victim of this break-in,” Michael Klotz, the chief executive of the branch, responded to the community meeting. One law firm representing a group of victims has claimed Sparkasse should be liable for all losses. “According to our information, there wasn’t even a motion or vibration detector inside the vault,” it claims on its website. Klotz countered that “all the burglar and fire alarm systems in our buildings were renewed two years ago”, but declined to go into detail. Besides, he concluded, “absolute security doesn’t exist”.

By early March, the police’s special organisational task force, codenamed “Bohrer” (“drill”) – which, at its peak, involved nearly 350 officers – had been streamlined to a “permanent investigation unit” of around 100 detectives. “When this crime was committed, a marathon began for the police,” says Nowaczyk. “We have already run more than half of it. But as anyone who has run a marathon knows, the last kilometres are the hardest – they feel longer.”

The force has received more than 600 tips, and approximately 50,000 items from the vault floor – the objects the gang rejected – remain subject to forensic analysis. Each piece is a potential source of DNA, fingerprints or tool marks. The gang had access to lavatories on the vault floor of the Sparkasse, and likely used them, given the amount of time they were inside the bank. “So going to the toilet was not a problem for them,” notes Nowaczyk. “Traceless? Yes, probably.”

Despite the scale of the task, Tim Frommeyer, the Gelsenkirchen chief of police, has expressed confidence about solving this case – due to the number of culprits, the equipment required, and the logistics of moving such a huge quantity of gold and cash around. “We will eventually catch these perpetrators. I am optimistic about that,” he has said.

For all its singular audacity, this heist fits a pattern. The “core drill”, of the kind used in construction work everywhere, has become a tool of choice for bank-breakers. “You won’t get a drill like this at the DIY store,” said a police spokesman soon after the robbery. Yet the Hilti DD 550-CA core drill, for example, is available to buy in Germany for around €10,000. Although heavy, it is relatively straightforward to operate with minimal training. Synthetic diamond-coated drill bits capable of making a hole of similar dimensions to the one in the side of the Gelsenkirchen vault are available to buy online with change from €600.

In 2021, robbers used a core drill to bore down into the vault of the Hamburger Sparkasse in Norderstedt, northern Germany, from the rented apartment above, taking the contents of around 650 safe deposit boxes and causing losses estimated at tens of millions of euros.

On Easter Monday in 2023, unknown perpetrators drilled through the rear wall of the Sparkasse Märkisch-Oderland in Strausberg, breaking into 301 of the 547 safe deposit boxes and making off with €12m in cash and valuables. A helicopter pursuit failed to catch the perpetrators.

The Gelsenkirchen drill hasn’t been found yet, but Nowaczyk is hopeful. “It could turn up on eBay, or dumped in the woods,” he muses.

Two months after the heist, on a sunny Tuesday morning in Gelsenkirchen, market stall holders selling handbags and vegetables remain mystified by the crime. The black Audi RS 6 with stolen Hanoverian plates (subsequently found in a bin near Dortmund’s main railway station) has not been traced to an owner – although according to a report in German news outlet Der Spiegel, investigators suspect that the perpetrators could be from the Netherlands. The looted money and gold – “geld und gold” as Nowaczyk puts it – has vanished into Europe’s criminal underground, or possibly farther afield. To date, no arrests have been made.

At the elegant, family-owned Weber jewellery store just a few doors down from the bank, manager Nora-Alena Weber fears “a clash between the cultures”, noting disapprovingly that she has heard some locals disparaging Turkish people in the wake of the robbery. “I still don’t know how this could happen,” she says.

Outside the Sparkasse, whose atrium remains open to a slow trickle of customers, a security guard stands sentry. He and his colleagues have occupied this post 24 hours a day, seven days a week since December 29: the bank that once felt no need for a constant human presence now cannot be left unwatched for a moment. Inside, there is another watchman: a giant red piggy bank occupies the middle of the floor. The lettering across its side reads “Sparkasse. Good for Gelsenkirchen.”

Some names have been changed

data.title: Miliband targets ‘limitless’ clean energy with Nottinghamshire fusion plant
data.description:
Ed Miliband has set out plans to target “virtually limitless” green energy with a nuclear fusion plant in Nottinghamshire.

The Government has appointed a consortium of construction companies to build a new fusion plant on the site of a former coal-fired power station in West Burton.

The ILIOS consortium, which is led by Kier and Nuvia, is expected to start construction in 2030, with the £200m build project scheduled to be completed by 2040.

Mr Miliband has made a £2.5bn bet on nuclear fusion as he seeks to reduce Britain’s reliance on fossil fuels and allow the country to generate its own clean energy.

This includes a shake-up of planning rules that will allow plants to be built in any part of Britain without the consent of local authorities.

Ministers added that the conflict in the Middle East and ensuing turmoil in the energy market underscored the need to ensure lasting energy abundance and security.

Fusion, which is the process that powers the sun, works by using extremely high temperatures and pressures to force hydrogen atoms to fuse, creating helium and releasing vast amounts of energy.

However, major challenges remain in controlling this process to produce energy in a safe and commercially viable way.

Miliband’s fusion energy drive

The Government previously put fusion at the heart of its industrial strategy as it seeks to make Britain the first country with a clear path to commercial fusion energy.

It has said this drive will support more than 10,000 jobs by the end of the decade, including in areas such as research and development.

Alongside the West Burton plant, the Government announced a £45m investment in the world’s most powerful fusion-dedicated AI supercomputer.

The supercomputer, called Sunrise, is aimed at tackling key challenges related to generating fusion energy while delivering benefits to other green energy technologies.

Sunrise, which is a collaboration between the UK Atomic Energy Authority and the University of Cambridge, is aiming to be operational by June.

The Government has also made a £20m cornerstone investment in Starmaker One, a UK fusion investment fund aimed at backing businesses and startups in the nascent sector.

Mr Miliband said: “From Nottinghamshire to Cumbria, and from the Oxford-Cambridge corridor to South Yorkshire, Britain has long been at the forefront of fusion energy.

“With our Fusion Strategy, we’re going further – backing industry, supporting over 10,000 jobs and paving the way for the ultimate long term energy security solution – clean, virtually limitless energy powered by British ingenuity and determination.”

Lord Vallance of Balham, the science minister, said: “We can be proud that Britain will lead the way on research, innovation and skills for a future of limitless fusion energy.

“By backing our fusion industry, we are not only securing our future energy independence, but from innovation and research to engineers, we are also providing the skilled clean energy jobs of the future for British people.”

data.title: Attack on Gail’s reopens anti-Semitism wounds at The Guardian
data.description:
Dozens gathered outside The Guardian’s glass-fronted headquarters on Wednesday, holding up banners and placards showing Nazis persecuting Jews and calling for an end to what they called “the new hate movement”.

Many also had a more unlikely symbol of protest: coffee cups and bags from Gail’s bakery.

The Guardian has been plunged into a fresh anti-Semitism storm amid claims the newspaper is minimising racism against Jews.

The title is struggling to contain the fallout from an opinion piece published over the weekend that downplayed repeated vandalism of a new branch of Gail’s in Archway, north London.

Founded by an Israeli baker in the 1990s, Gail’s has been targeted by pro-Palestinian activists because its largest shareholder is Bain Capital, a private equity firm that also invests in Israeli defence and cybersecurity firms.

Jonathan Liew, the columnist who wrote the article, described the vandalism of the Archway cafe as “small acts of petty symbolism”, a phrase that has sparked an internal backlash from staff and criticism from politicians.

Eve Kay, a longstanding Archway resident who attended the protest in north London, accused The Guardian of being “caught up in an anti-Semitic, anti Zionist mindset”.

‘They’ve lost their moral and political bearings’

She said: “That means that they can’t really see that there is a huge avalanche of racism, a tsunami of racism against Jews, and so they find it hard to acknowledge and to report on, and they don’t even realise when they’re doing it themselves.

“I think they’ve lost their moral and political bearings, quite frankly.”

Mark Birbeck, the founder of campaign group Our Fight and one of those at the protest at The Guardian’s HQ at King’s Cross on Wednesday, said the paper was the “acceptable end of a very dangerous trend” where legitimate concerns about anti-Semitism were shut down.

He added: “The problem I think we have here is that the Guardian doesn’t recognise that, it is not accepting that, and so most of its coverage seems to be, in my view, biased, and covering up this anti-Semitism in plain sight.”

The article has piled renewed pressure on editor Katharine Viner to act. Under her editorship, the paper has been embroiled in a number of anti-Semitism scandals, including cartoons of Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu and former BBC chairman Richard Sharp.

Jay Rayner, the former Guardian restaurant critic who quit after 28 years in 2024, accused the newspaper of employing anti-Semites and Ms Viner of lacking the courage to take them on.

Josh Howie, a GB News presenter who helped organise the protest, said: “This is just the latest minimisation of anti-Semitism from The Guardian and I think the Jewish community are feeling very beleaguered.

“The Guardian, which is a paper that I used to write for, who are ostensibly anti-racism, have spent years not just minimising racism, but arguably actually sanitising it.”

So far, Viner has not commented and The Guardian has said complaints about its journalism are considered by the internally independent readers’ editor under its editorial code and guidance.

Guardian warns staff of protest

The Guardian sent an urgent email to staff about the protest on Tuesday night, with security diverting employees away from the main entrance.

However, it has now edited the article to remove the phrase “small acts of petty symbolism” in reference to the vandalism, which it said was to “avoid misunderstanding”.

It also moved a sentence accusing Gail’s of “heavy-handed high-street aggression” after opening a site close to a Palestinian cafe.

That came after the Conservative Party leader Kemi Badenoch branded the article “a cover for disgusting anti-Semitism”.

Mr Liew, a sports journalist who also writes columns, has stirred controversy with previous articles about Israel and the football club Maccabi Tel Aviv, as well as a row over transgender athletes taking part in Parkrun’s female category.

His latest column came after the Archway branch of Gail’s was attacked twice in the space of a week by vandals who smashed windows and graffitied messages including “reject corporate Zionism”.

Sir James Cleverly, the shadow housing secretary, has submitted a parliamentary question demanding to know whether Labour intends to do anything to stop further anti-Semitic attacks against Gail’s branches.

He told The Telegraph: “The attack on Gail’s is nothing more than a cover for anti-Semitism. This vile hatred must be stamped out.

“Labour has done next to nothing about this, and is too busy pandering to the politics of sectarianism. So I am raising the matter in Parliament to try and force ministers to act.”

A government spokesman said: “Antisemitism is abhorrent and has no place in our society. The Government remains committed to tackling all forms of hatred and ensuring that everyone in the UK can live free from fear of discrimination or violence.

“Where incidents involve criminal behaviour, this is an operational matter for the police.”

Meanwhile, the Board of Deputies of British Jews believes readers deserve to know why The Guardian piece was published in the first place.

A spokesman said: “It is not acceptable to refer to the opening of a bakery as an act of ‘aggression’ on the basis of the ethnic, religious or national ties of its founders, or to justify importing conflict onto our streets on the basis of those characteristics.

“Most people will find this article, seeped in tropes and innuendos, as deeply insidious, and will want to know why the Guardian thinks an op-ed seemingly justifying tensions between communities has a place on its pages.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Chris Boffey,  intrepid Fleet Street newsman who crossed the globe in pursuit of stories
data.description:
Chris Boffey, who has died aged 74, was head of news at The Sunday Telegraph and one of the most versatile and accomplished journalists of his generation.

Boffey was recruited by The Sunday Telegraph as chief reporter in 1997 following a spell as head of news at the Sunday Mirror, where he fell out with the editor Bridget Rowe when she ordered surveillance on the home of David Blunkett, then a member of Labour’s shadow cabinet, because she “did not believe he was really blind”.

As chief reporter, Boffey covered the 1998 World Cup hooligan riots in Marseilles at such close range that his photographer was injured by a bottle to the head. A month later, Boffey was in Omagh reporting from the scene of a Real IRA bombing in which 29 were killed and 220 wounded.

Swiftly promoted to head of news, Boffey was always ready with a touch of humour. During one editorial conference, he announced: “There are a thousand stories in this city – and we haven’t got any of them.” A carefully timed pause, with the editor looking increasingly concerned, was followed by: “But we will have by the end of the week.”

Christopher John Boffey was born on November 23 1951 in Wythenshawe, Manchester, the youngest of three children of William Boffey, a print worker at the Kemsley House printworks, and his wife Nora, née Daley, a waitress.

Known as “the Fleet Street of the North”, Kemsley House printed the northern editions of most national newspapers and Boffey pored over the copies that his father brought home. “From the age of 11, I was determined to be a reporter who travelled the world.”

Boffey became a cub reporter with the Winsford Guardian, 15 miles from his home, before joining a larger regional paper, The Journal in Newcastle, and then the Daily Star. It was the beginning of a long journey which would realise his childhood ambitions to travel the world and see his byline on an astonishing range of stories in virtually all the leading British newspapers – while frequently being in danger.

While in Cyprus for the Daily Star, tracking an escaped IRA terrorist, Boffey was forced to the ground with a gun to his head by a Cypriot police officer who believed him to be the terrorist. He was in Northern Ireland in 1988 for The Sun covering the funeral of three IRA terrorists when a fellow hack dragged him behind a gravestone as the loyalist gunman Michael Stone opened fire, killing three and wounding 60 others.

During the 1990 Gulf War, now with the News of the World, Boffey was embedded aboard the destroyer HMS Cardiff. He was woken at 3am by a chemical weapons alert and rushed up on deck wearing only his gas mask, which, he said, “broke the ice”.

He was also one of the first journalists to expose the sexual offences of Gary Glitter, and his discovery of a former nanny to Fred and Rose West helped secure Rose West’s conviction.

In 2001 Boffey left The Sunday Telegraph when his friend Alastair Campbell, director of communications for the Prime Minister Tony Blair, persuaded him to become a special adviser to Estelle Morris, then Education Secretary. “Chris was utterly unflappable,” she said. “His advice was first-class, and when I think of him, it is his cheery, chuckling face that first comes to mind.”

When she resigned, Boffey went to The Daily Telegraph as a reporter then to the Mirror as head of news. He briefly worked for The Sunday Times and The Independent on Sunday before joining The Observer. His last job in newspapers was on the Guardian news desk, where his risqué humour caused occasional palpitations.

In retirement, he chaired the Journalists’ Charity for several years.

He is survived by his wife Shirley, a retired headteacher, whom he married in 1972, and by their sons Daniel, chief reporter at The Guardian, and Martin, a lawyer.

Chris Boffey, born November 23 1951, died February 13 2026

data.title: The nightmare scenario for energy markets has become reality
data.description:
There is a scenario that energy professionals and policymakers have quietly dreaded for years. Hormuz closed. Ras Laffan hit. The largest natural gas field on Earth, disrupted. This week, it stopped being a scenario. We have woken up and are living this nightmare today.

The instinct of a British reader – and I understand it – is to see this as a Gulf story. Wealthy states, distant conflict, rising oil prices: unfortunate, but essentially someone else’s problem. I want to explain, as precisely as I can, why that instinct is wrong – why its ramifications really matter to the UK.

The gas that heats millions of British homes begins its journey in Qatar’s North Field – the largest natural gas reservoir on earth. It is liquefied, loaded onto tankers, shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, and eventually piped into British boilers thousands of miles away. I know that route well – I was part of the team that developed the South Hook liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminal in South Wales, one of Europe’s largest import facilities, purpose-built to carry Qatari gas into British homes. It has operated without a single supply disruption since it opened. That reflects a relationship between two countries built on trust, long-term commitment, and shared interest.

That relationship runs deeper than energy. Britain and Qatar have spent decades building ties across diplomacy, finance, education and culture. When Russia cut gas supplies to Europe in 2022, Qatar helped plug the gap. The diversification that insulated Britain from the worst of that shock was not an accident. It was the result of relationships and infrastructure built over years, sustained through a partnership unheralded until it matters enormously. Relying on Qatar was not a failure of imagination – it was sound policy. Qatar is among the most dependable supply partners any country could have: a proven supplier, diplomatic and a long-term ally. The lesson of the Russia crisis wasn’t to rely less on Qatar. It was to protect the conditions that guarantee reliability.

Qatar was not party to this conflict. It specialises in diplomatic mediation, hosting negotiations and advocating de-escalation. Its people and tens of thousands of international workers who power its infrastructure didn’t choose this. Neither did the family in Blackpool whose heating bill is about to rise, nor the Midlands manufacturer facing soaring energy costs. That’s the impact of an attack on critical infrastructure.

Damage that began in the Gulf is reverberating across Britain. Gas is not a peripheral concern for the UK economy; it is structural. It accounts for 36pc of the UK’s total primary energy supply and 64pc of household energy consumption. It heats the majority of British homes, powers industrial processes from ceramics to chemicals, and the sector supports 115,000 jobs across supply chains, terminals and offshore.

When gas prices spike, the consequences aren’t abstract. They’re a direct hit on family budgets, factory costs and business margins. The volatility Britain endured after 2022 was a preview of what global supply disruption looks like in practice. But this version is sharper, and here’s what makes it acute. Britain holds just 10 to 16 days of gas in reserve. There is no significant buffer, no comforting margin for error.

LNG isn’t the only answer to Britain’s long-term energy needs. Transition to cleaner energy is essential, and Qatar understands this. It is investing in that future too by committing hundreds of millions of pounds to UK climate tech and nuclear energy. But transition takes decades, and LNG currently accounts for 20pc of UK gas supply. Lose access to flexible, reliable LNG, and you create a gap that no current alternative can fill at speed or scale.

Families anxiously watching their thermostat and manufacturers absorbing rising costs didn’t cause this crisis, but they’re enduring its consequences. Behind every bill, every order book and every stressful calculation, lies a chain of energy relationships that most people don’t think about until crisis strikes. For Britain, Qatar is the linchpin of that chain and a partner that’s never sought recognition.

This precious relationship, built on trust not transaction, is manifested quietly in warm homes and humming factories rather than loud headlines. Qatar has stood by Britain before and will do so again. But this moment asks something of the rest of us. To understand that a partnership this deep and this significant deserves more than goodwill to preserve it. We have every right to expect concrete international frameworks and enforceable protections for the infrastructure on which our way of life depends. The relationship doesn’t need defending. What it requires is a world that finally understands its importance and acts accordingly.

Omran Al-Kuwari is a fellow at the Atlantic Council Global Energy Centre and Honorary Senior research fellow at University College London (UCL) Bartlett School of Energy Environment and Resources

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: As the world burns, Lloyd’s of London is missing in action
data.description:
On the face of it, things couldn’t be rosier at Lloyd’s of London, the £50bn insurance behemoth that provides cover for everything from floods and wildfires to cyber attacks, terrorism and even top celebrities.

Annual profits are up by £1bn to a stonking £10.6bn, gross written premiums increased 4.2pc to £57.9bn last year and the market’s balance sheet is strong, with total capital and reserves jumping 5.7pc to £49.8bn.

In the mundane world of insurance, this apparently counts for “a firm foundation for the challenges and risks ahead”, Patrick Tiernan, the recently anointed chief executive, said.

Yet behind the somewhat cheery sound-bites, something is amiss. In its annual report published on Thursday morning, just hours after the Middle East was turned into a giant inferno, the crisis barely gets a mention.

Worse, Tiernan appears to be avoiding the mainstream media, having hand-picked a handful of newswires and specialist publications to speak to while shunning The Telegraph and its rivals. As the world burns, is its most important insurance market missing in action?

After all, this is the same business that has faced accusations of pricing ship owners out of the market by jacking up premiums at a time when cover has never been more critical.

These are serious, legitimate concerns that need to be properly addressed – is Lloyd’s open for business for shipping and marine insurance in the Middle East or not?

It’s fine for Sir Charles Roxburgh, the chairman, to enthuse in his preamble to the report about how his goal is “to fulfil our vision for Lloyd’s to be the world’s pre-eminent global marketplace for insurance risk”, but the test of that is during mega-crises like these.

Ditto, Tiernan’s breezy statement about Lloyd’s of London benefiting “from 337 years of lived experience”. “It has endured countless wars, multiple financial crises and at least four industrial revolutions,” he says.

But what is the position today regarding what some experts believe could prove to be a more serious economic crisis than the oil shock of 1973?

Lloyd’s of London’s annual report stretches to 172 pages, six of which are dedicated to climate-related financial disclosures, 11 to executive pay, while “purpose” gets a whole page of its own.

Yet the situation in the Middle East gets nothing more than a passing mention, and where it is dealt with it’s either in the context of “earthquakes” or “human consequences”. All of this is no doubt important but clients want to know what the commercial consequences are.

The publication of the report – just hours after Tehran’s targeting of the Ras Laffan liquefied natural gas plant effectively turned the conflict from a military one into an economic one – was nothing more than unfortunate timing on Lloyd’s part, of course.

Nevertheless, it makes the failure to address the war all the more glaring. There isn’t even a mention of the Strait of Hormuz, the key waterway where the company at the heart of global maritime cover has faced criticism over cancelled policies and sharp price rises.

Lloyd’s of London will no doubt point to an interview that Roxburgh gave a week ago following an emergency meeting with the Chancellor where discussions were held about the part insurers could play in helping to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Yet his statement was too vague and non-committal to amount to much. “I reiterated Lloyd’s confidence in our marine insurance market, which has remained open and continues to support international trade and shipping during this period of heightened risk,” Roxburgh said.

No doubt he was also under pressure from the Treasury to provide some reassuring words given all the uncertainty. Besides, the situation in the Gulf has deteriorated markedly since then. Clarification is needed.

What’s more, despite the insurance giant’s insistence that it has not stopped providing contracts to those who want them, it is clear that few shipping companies – if any – are seeking them.

Many of course are simply too terrified of running the gauntlet of Iranian mines and submarines. That seems to be Tiernan’s argument. Shippers seeking insurance for tankers going through the strait “is pretty rare at the moment because people are focused on safety and security”, he said.

But the rocketing price of insurance must surely be a deterrent too. “It’s our base case that all ships currently in the Gulf will have had their policies cancelled, and almost all of these will have reinstated it at the new (much higher) price,” analysts said.

The broker Marsh estimates that insurance rates for physical damage to a vessel from war have risen to as much as 1.5pc of the ship’s insured value from 0.25pc – a sixfold increase – before the Iran conflict began.

With oil tankers valued at anywhere between $17m (£13m) and $100m in 2025, shipping companies face having to fork out hundreds of thousands of dollars more per voyage. In some cases, it could mean an additional $1m per ship.

So yes, in the words of its chairman, Lloyd’s effectively “continues to support international trade and shipping” – but if the cost has become too prohibitive, then, for all intents and purposes, the market may be deemed to be as good as closed.

The situation is given greater urgency by the scrutiny of a certain US president who is prone to lashing out against those he perceives to be inhibiting his war, rather than helping it.

Donald Trump’s offer of $20bn of maritime reinsurance for the region may well prove to be little more than his usual rhetoric, but he knows how to hold a grudge and may not forget this perceived slight easily.

Either way, it’s clear Lloyd’s reputation has taken a knock, threatening to undermine its status as one of Britain’s pre-eminent financial institutions.

Tiernan has been in the job for 10 months so may still be finding his feet, but there’s no excuse for hiding.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Serve warmer beer to save on bills, Miliband tells pubs
data.description:
Pubs should serve warmer beer and turn off ovens to save money on their energy bills, Ed Miliband has said.

The Energy Secretary has launched an advice tool aimed at hospitality businesses, which he hopes will ease the burden of rising costs in the struggling sector.

The tool encourages firms to reduce unnecessary electricity use by turning off bottle fridges overnight and to monitor hotspots such as extraction systems, ovens and lamps.

It comes as fears mount that more pubs are being pushed to the brink, as the conflict in Iran sends energy prices soaring.

Analysis by The Telegraph last weekend suggested that the spike in energy bills will heap an extra £169m a year onto pubs’ costs, with Wetherspoons chief Sir Tim Martin saying businesses would have no choice but to push up prices.

Businesses claim they are being quoted energy rates around 30pc higher than in February, before the US and Israel launched their first strikes on Iran. Oil prices have risen from $73 (£55) a barrel before the strikes to hover around $100.

‘Eye-watering bills’

Industry leaders on Tuesday said the government tool would not save pubs from the “eye-watering bills” that were crippling hospitality businesses, with landlords ridiculing the suggestions as “groundbreaking stuff”.

Emma McClarkin, the chief executive of the British Beer and Pub Association, said: “There are a host of appliances that you simply cannot turn off, many for health and safety reasons, so it is not just help with reducing eye-watering energy bills that the beer and pub sector needs the Government to help with, but the overall cumulative costs of doing business, including disproportionate tax bills.”

However, she said: “With the typical pub making just 12p profit on every £5 pint, it’s essential for landlords to save money on their energy bills wherever they can, with the added bonus of reducing their carbon footprint.”

A trial of the tool was overseen by tech firm Carbon Zero Services last year.

Mark Holden, the company’s chief executive, said: “When you put energy savings into real hospitality terms, the impact becomes very clear.

“Saving around £2,000 a year is the equivalent of the profit from selling thousands of pints or the breathing space that protects hard-won margins during quieter months.”

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero claimed that the government-funded carbon-reduction tool had already saved trial participants £48 a week and hopes to expand the trial to 525 additional businesses.

It follows growing calls for Labour to offer more support for pubs. The Telegraph has been campaigning for the Government to reduce the sector’s cost burden by cutting taxes and slashing red tape.

Pub owner Andy Lennox urged ministers to slash VAT rates for British hospitality firms rather than offer them simplistic energy-saving suggestions.

Mr Lennox ran a campaign last year to ban Labour MPs from hospitality venues across the country, which ultimately forced Labour to announce an emergency support package for pubs.

He said: “To be told to turn the lights off overnight really is groundbreaking stuff. Thank goodness someone in Whitehall finally cracked it. Decades of hospitality experience across the country, and the answer was sitting there all along.

“In reality, this is yet another short-sighted, bureaucratic, headline-grabbing load of rubbish. Any half-decent operator already runs an efficient kitchen, manages energy properly and watches costs like a hawk. That is Hospitality 101.”

‘Clean power mission’

Chris McDonald, the industry minister, said that soaring oil prices were evidence that Britain needed to accelerate its net zero transition to shield businesses from global price shocks.

He added: “We’re extending support to help more businesses slash bills and protect Britain’s hospitality sector.

“Our pubs and restaurants are playing a leading role in cutting emissions, which is not only good for the planet, but for cutting their costs too.

“Alongside this, our clean power mission will protect businesses in the long term. The conflict in the Middle East is yet another reminder that the only route to energy sovereignty is to end our dependence on fossil fuel markets we do not control and transition to clean, home-grown power.”

The advice given on Labour’s new tool will invite comparisons with the Conservative government’s much-maligned household energy-saving drive.

At the height of the energy crisis triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, former energy secretary Jacob Rees-Mogg drew up a £15m campaign to encourage households to use less energy.

However, the plan was rejected by then-prime minister Liz Truss, who was understood to have found the idea “nannying”.

The “It All Adds Up” campaign later re-emerged under Rishi Sunak’s government, costing the taxpayer £18m.

At the time, households were offered simple energy-saving tips such as turning down boiler settings to 60°C, switching off standby appliances and washing clothes at lower temperatures.

Some of the advice, which included taking shorter showers, was widely pilloried at a time when household bills were reaching all-time highs.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The Iran crisis will be the nail in the coffin for British manufacturing
data.description:
“It’s moments like this that tell you what a government is about,” the Prime Minister claimed at a news conference in Downing Street on Monday with absolutely no sense of irony whatsoever.

With crushing predictability, the crisis in the Middle East has served as another reminder – as if one was needed – that this Labour Government is so far out of its depth, inflatable arm bands may have to become a compulsory item at future Cabinet meetings.

Sir Keir Starmer and co have been caught completely on the back foot by both the speed and gravity of the situation in Iran. In the search for answers to the cost of living crisis that is about to barrel through every home across the country, ministers have taken the easy option, turning this into a fight against perceived profiteering by big business.

But don’t be fooled. Tasking the Competition and Markets Authority with conducting a review into suspicions of price gouging at the pumps is classic Labour.

It’s nothing more than posturing. By pointing the finger at an imaginary bogeyman, the Government is attempting to distract from real issues such as how its needless opposition to North Sea oil and gas has made Britain more vulnerable to energy shocks.

Worse, while Sir Keir and Rachel Reeves play at being the guardians of working people, the threat to Britain’s industrial base is in danger of being overlooked.

It is, however, no exaggeration to say that the Iran crisis could easily accelerate the death of manufacturing if the vicious spike in oil and gas prices lasts longer than a few weeks.

American chemicals tycoon Peter Huntsman has become the latest to sound the alarm with a threat to join the growing line of foreign investors that have abandoned the UK over sky-high energy costs.

The Huntsman Corporation is the owner of one of the few remaining slices of what was Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), Britain’s largest manufacturer for much of the 20th century – a factory producing aniline in Wilton, Teesside.

Aniline is critical to car and aircraft manufacturing, clothing production, the pharmaceuticals industry, agriculture, furniture-making, and other big sectors. It helps make car tyres and aircraft parts. It is also used as a dyeing agent for the manufacturing of clothes like jeans, in the production of drugs, such as paracetamol, and in fertilisers and pesticides.

Yet Huntsman, whose father built the family business into a global chemicals empire, says that if elevated energy prices “stay in place for the next three months, I would shut down my [UK] facility and I’d be importing products from China or the United States.”

It is a far cry from only four years ago when aniline prices in the UK were cheaper than the rest of the world. “Right now, this week, it is the most expensive,” said Huntsman.

It is a scenario that has become all-too familiar. Sky-high energy costs are crushing the life out of heavy industry. Production lines are being shut down and entire factory complexes mothballed altogether, leaving the country at real risk of widespread de-industrialisation.

The Chancellor has been warned repeatedly that eye-watering bills are the primary reason why swathes of heavy industry have been forced to close. Yet she has failed to do anything meaningful about it.

Opinion is bitterly divided on the causes, but it is a fact that the UK has some of the most expensive industrial energy prices in the developed world. Bills are almost two-thirds above the median of International Energy Agency countries and the highest among G7 members.

This isn’t a new phenomenon either. Electricity costs for businesses remain 70pc higher than before Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, while gas prices are 60pc higher, according to a recent report from Energy UK, which represents more than 100 electricity generators and retailers.

Nor can the impact be disputed: almost 90pc of companies have seen energy bills rise over the last five years and four in ten have reduced investment as a result, the report also found. Against this grim backdrop, it is hardly a coincidence that Britain’s trade deficit for goods leapt by £30.5bn to £248.3bn last year – the worst on record.

Environmental campaigners say the root cause is Britain’s heavy reliance on gas, which currently sets the price of electricity 98pc of the time. Obtain a greater proportion from renewable energy and nuclear reactors, and electricity costs would magically begin to fall as gas use dwindles, goes the argument of net zero proponents.

But what the green brigade conveniently overlooks is the exorbitant costs of the clean energy transition. British manufacturers face some of the highest carbon taxes in the world – taxes that are making them less competitive – along with a slew of green levies that account for nearly of the average energy bill.

These costs are killing off British factories in ever greater numbers, undermining our ability to produce food, equip the armed forces, and build critical infrastructure.

Mostly, the Government has done little more than stand and watch. The impression is that ministers, led by the economic illiterate Ed Miliband, are content to see this take place because it means fewer emissions on the path to net zero.

That, however, is a giant red herring. As industrialists including Ineos’s Jim Ratcliffe point out, “decarbonising by de-industrialisation” not only means the loss of UK jobs and expertise, but a greater reliance on foreign imports that come with a heavier environmental footprint and make us more vulnerable.

Even now, the Government is sticking to the net zero script and blaming “the fossil fuel penalty” for the problems at Huntsman’s Teesside plant.

Regardless of the cause, what cannot be disputed is that manufacturing is being hollowed out at a frightening pace. Where it has stepped in to prevent the loss of critical capabilities, Labour has tended to leave it until the 11th hour, forcing taxpayers to foot the bill. It is why the state is now a shareholder in Ineos’s ethylene plant in Grangemouth, along with steelworks in Scunthorpe, Rotherham and Sheffield.

But this is not a coherent approach either. The latest iteration of the industrial strategy gave grounds for hope with the promise of energy bill reductions for energy intensive sectors through a “British industrial competitiveness scheme”.

However, that was in July last year, and the measures don’t kick in until 2027 by which time more factories will almost certainly have been forced out of business.

With the Prime Minister refusing to rule out fuel rationing, the case for bringing forward support is overwhelming.

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.
