data.title: North Sea giant ready to exploit UK’s biggest oil field this year
data.description:
Britain’s largest oil field could be producing thousands of barrels a day by the autumn if Ed Miliband approves the North Sea project, its co-owner has said.

Ithaca Energy, which owns the Rosebank site off Shetland, on Wednesday said it was poised to get oil flowing within months but the Energy Secretary first needed to sign off on the plan.

Rosebank, 60 miles west of Shetland, is the North Sea’s largest undeveloped site, which could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil and gas.

However, Mr Miliband has been a fierce critic of the plan, branding it climate vandalism.

He now faces an energy crisis and growing political calls to approve such schemes, piling pressure on him to approve Rosebank.

Donald Trump, the US president, again lashed out at Britain on Tuesday for not taking more advantage of the North Sea, having previously urged the UK Government to restart drilling.

Ithaca and Rosebank’s other co-owner Adura have invested more than £3bn in the project with a giant production vessel, the Petrojarl Rosebank, now rounding Africa and due to reach Scottish waters next month.

Ithaca described Rosebank as “entering the final stages of development towards first production in 2026-27” in its latest financial results on Wednesday.

It also announced plans to start producing oil and gas from Cambo, another controversial oil and gas field almost as large as Rosebank, and from Tornado, a mainly gas field. They too will need Mr Miliband’s consent.

Yaniv Friedman, Ithaca’s executive chairman, said: “In the West of Shetland, we saw material project activity, including at Rosebank where we are progressing in line with our development timeline towards first production in 2026-27.

“We also saw significant progress on the maturation of the Cambo and Tornado projects, with all activities supporting a potential final investment decision within 12 months.”

Oil from Rosebank would be exported because the UK now lacks the refinery capacity to process it. But it would generate UK jobs and billions in taxes as well as boosting north European energy supplies.

Rosebank’s gas output would enter UK pipelines, amounting to around 1pc of national needs. The Cambo and Tornado fields lie nearby and their gas output would also enter UK pipes.

Mr Miliband would be likely to face a backlash from Labour backbenchers and climate campaigners should he approve them. All already have licences, granted by the previous government, but lack final development consents.

The decision is meant to be made on technical rather than political grounds – Adura and Ithaca must show the field meets new criteria on overall emissions from producing and using the oil and gas.

Rising pressure on Miliband

However, the looming local and regional elections in May, including for the Welsh and Scottish parliaments, also mean any decision will be carefully timed for maximum political advantage.

Andrew Bowie, the shadow secretary of state for Scotland, called on Mr Miliband to approve such projects.

Mr Bowie said: “Thousands of jobs and millions of pounds in revenue are being missed out on every day that goes past while Ed Miliband allows his eco zealotry to trump the national interest.

“The crisis caused by the closing of the Straits of Hormuz and wider impact on oil and gas exports from the Gulf demonstrate more than ever before the necessity of maxing out our domestic supply of energy from the North Sea.”

Ithaca, majority owned by Israel’s Delek and Italy’s Eni, is now one of the UK’s largest offshore operators, but has been hit hard by the North Sea windfall levy which has pushed industry taxes on oil and gas profits to 78pc.

It made pre-tax profits of $840m (£629m) but was hit by the levy and again by Labour’s decision to extend it from 2028 to 2030 – forcing it to set aside an extra $327m.

Iain Lewis, Ithaca’s chief financial officer, said the company had been hit by a tax rate of 110pc resulting in a post-tax loss of $84m.

Separately, Norwegian state-controlled energy giant Equinor has announced a string of new oil and gas discoveries in Norway’s North Sea “Sleipnir” area, close to the UK border, from which gas is piped to Britain.

Cecilie Rønning, from Equinor, said: “Sleipner is an important hub for gas exports, and we must do everything we can to identify the remaining resources. We plan to drill three additional exploration wells and two new production wells in the area this year.”

data.title: Boss of £2bn shadow bank accused of fraud has assets frozen
data.description:
The owner of a £2bn shadow bank accused of “very serious” fraud has been hit with a worldwide asset-freezing order.

Administrators of Market Financial Solutions (MFS) have secured a worldwide freezing order against Paresh Raja, the private credit company’s owner, after it collapsed last month.

Mr Raja must provide details of all his assets worth more than £10,000 under the order and is prevented from spending more than £5,000 a week without the consent of the administrators, people familiar with the matter told The Telegraph.

Courts in London and Dubai both ratified the applications made by AlixPartners and both orders have been served on Mr Raja. The news was first reported by the Financial Times.

AlixPartners said: “We welcome the granting of these applications which follow two weeks of intense analysis and investigation into the operations and affairs of MFS and Paresh Raja.

“This is an important and significant step in this very complex situation, and the support of the courts is critical as we continue our pursuit of the best possible outcome for all creditors of both MFS and its associated companies.”

MFS was placed into administration late last month amid what a judge called “very serious” allegations of fraud.

Mr Raja has been accused of using a network of fake companies to steal money from the company’s creditors. A legal filing from creditors claimed that some MFS clients presented as “genuine borrowers” were in fact “closely connected with” MFS.

The allegations centre on eight companies that borrowed from MFS. Creditors of MFS successfully placed these eight companies into administration last Tuesday.

Six of the eight are owned by two individuals connected to Magus Chartered Accountancy, a small London-based firm that acted as accountants for MFS. There is no suggestion of wrongdoing by Magus.

In their claim, the creditors said that lending to connected borrowers, as well as the “double-pledging” of collateral to secure multiple loans, had left a hole in MFS’s accounts of more than £1.3bn.

Lawyers for Mr Raja have rejected the creditors’ allegations, stating that there had been no intention to defraud and asserting that Mr Raja had not been the beneficiary of any shortfall if there had been one.

The collapse of MFS has fuelled concerns about the so-called shadow bank market following a series of high-profile collapses in the US last year. Shadow banks are private credit providers that are not subject to the same government regulations as high street lenders.

As a shadow bank, MFS did not take deposits and instead funded its loans by borrowing from itself. A cast of high-profile banks – including Santander, Wells Fargo, Jefferies and Barclays – backed MFS before its collapse.

Speaking at a Morgan Stanley conference on Wednesday, CS Venkatakrishnan, the chief executive Barclays, said MFS appeared to be a “fairly deep and sophisticated fraud”.

“Obviously I’m disappointed we had anything to do with [it]”, he added.

Its demise not only shocked the City but has prompted the Bank of England to investigate.

Representatives for Mr Raja declined to comment on the freezing order.

data.title: Collapsed shadow bank under investigation by City watchdog
data.description:
The City watchdog has launched an investigation into a £2bn shadow bank that collapsed amid allegations of fraud.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) said on Friday that it had launched an inquiry into Market Financial Solutions (MFS) over its compliance with money laundering and terrorist financing rules.

The regulator did not provide any further comment on the investigation.

It comes after MFS was placed into administration late last month amid what a judge called “very serious” allegations of fraud.

MFS was a so-called shadow bank, which meant it did not accept deposits and instead funded its loans by borrowing from itself.

A series of high-profile banks, including Santander, Wells Fargo, Jefferies and Barclays,  have been stung after lending hundreds of millions of pounds to MFS.

The company described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance.

It was part of a fast-growing crop of so-called bridging lenders in the UK. These firms provide short-term, property-backed loans to borrowers who may not qualify for traditional bank financing and often charge higher interest rates.

Its demise not only shocked the City but has also prompted the Bank of England to investigate the circumstances of the collapse.

The private credit provider’s owner, Paresh Raja, has also been accused by some of the company’s creditors of using a network of fake companies to steal money from the company. Mr Raja declined to comment on the FCA investigation.

A legal filing from creditors claimed that some MFS clients presented as “genuine borrowers” were in fact “closely connected with” the shadow bank.

In their claim, the creditors said that lending to connected borrowers, as well as the “double-pledging” of collateral to secure multiple loans, had left a hole in MFS’s accounts of more than £1.3bn.

Lawyers for Mr Raja have rejected the creditors’ allegations, stating that there had been no intention to defraud and asserting that Mr Raja had not been the beneficiary of any shortfall if there had been one.

Administrators overseeing MFS secured a worldwide freezing order against Mr Raja on Thursday.

He must now provide details of all his assets worth more than £10,000 under the order and is prevented from spending more than £5,000 a week without the consent of administrators.

Speaking at a Morgan Stanley conference on Wednesday, CS Venkatakrishnan, the chief executive of Barclays, said MFS appeared to be a “fairly deep and sophisticated fraud”.

“Obviously, I’m disappointed we had anything to do with [it]”, he said.

data.title: ‘It’s a shame’: US mustard maker in talks to buy Colman’s and Marmite
data.description:
The US maker of French’s yellow mustard is in talks to buy Marmite and Colman’s, making them the latest British brands that could fall into American hands.

Unilever, which owns the spread and sauce, said it had received an offer for its food division from Baltimore-based spice and sauce maker McCormick.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from the Bass brewery. Colman’s was founded in Norwich in 1814 and is still made from mustard seeds grown in the area.

The food industry reacted with dismay to news of the potential sale, following other US takeovers of historic brands such as Cadbury and Lea & Perrins.

John Farrand, the managing director of the Guild of Fine Food, called the potential sale “sad because that’s removing revenue and business from the UK”.

He said: “These producers get amalgamated into big venture capital funds, and once the accountants get hold of them, they become a number on a spreadsheet.”

Jim Winship, of the British Sandwich and Food to Go Association, said: “It’s a shame, in a way, that we lose our brands, but we live in a global world where these things happen all the time.

“We’re not very good at keeping innovations, so perhaps we’re a bit remiss as a country for not protecting our own.”

News of the offer follows weeks of speculation that Unilever had been in discussions with Kraft Heinz to combine their food operations.

In a statement posted on Friday, the company said it believed that its food division, which also includes Hellmann’s Mayonnaise and Knorr stock cubes, remained a “highly attractive business, with a strong financial profile led by market-leading brands in growing categories”.

It said it was “confident in the future of the goods business as part of Unilever”. However, it said it had received an offer from McCormick & Co and was currently in talks with the US giant.

As well as French’s, a sweet mustard typically associated with hot dogs, McCormick also makes Frank’s Red Hot and Cholula hot sauces and various spice blends, including Old Bay seasoning.

Unilever said there was “no certainty any transaction will be agreed”. Its food business is estimated to be worth roughly $36bn (£27bn). A sale would leave the company focused on home and beauty brands such as Dove, Vaseline and Lynx.

Food shake-up

Unilever recently demerged its ice cream business, spinning it out as The Magnum Ice Cream Company.

The new entity, which was the result of an almost two-year-long process, is now home to Ben & Jerry’s and Cornetto, as well as the eponymous ice cream brand.

Unilever has been on a major drive to trim down its sprawling portfolio of food brands.

It sold its spreads business in 2017, which included I Can’t Believe it’s not Butter and Flora, and in 2022, it offloaded most of its tea business, including Lipton and PG Tips.

Fernando Fernandez, the chief executive, who took the reins of the company in March last year, recently said the company would continue to carve off brands.

In Unilever’s most recent annual report, Mr Fernandez outlined a vision for the company to achieve 65pc of its sales from beauty, wellness and personal care.

He told a conference in New York earlier this year: “We are really shifting our portfolio into more beauty, more wellbeing, more personal care.”

Unilever’s share price rose slightly following the announcement. However, analysts at Barclays said speculation over the future of the company’s food business was unhelpful.

In a note published on Friday, analysts said: “Unilever has been very clear that its future lies in beauty and wellbeing and not in foods. Exiting ice cream was about doing fewer things better with more impact.”

However, they said management had appeared to indicate at its last financial results that “an exit from foods wasn’t something on the table in the short term and even when it might be, it would need to be a compelling and value accretive option”.

The note by analysts at Barclays added: “Unlike previous CEO Hein Schumacher, who would have probably been more precise about the future of foods, it has always seemed that Mr Fernandez was more open-minded and less inclined to close down speculation about the long term future of its foods business.”

data.title: Strip ex-Environment Agency chief of knighthood, say water campaigners
data.description:
Campaigners are calling for the former chief executive of the Environment Agency to be stripped of his knighthood, claiming he failed to combat sewage pollution while in post.

A petition calling for Sir James Bevan to be stripped of the honour has attracted almost 5,000 signatures since it was launched last week.

Sir James, who ran the Environment Agency from 2015 until March 2023, has been criticised for overseeing a reduction in water quality monitoring and accused of gagging staff from speaking out about the state of Britain’s rivers.

His track record came under scrutiny in the Channel 4 docuseries, Dirty Business, which aired earlier this month.

The series depicts the veteran mandarin as praising “operator self-monitoring”, meaning water companies were effectively allowed to mark their own homework on pollution rather than Environment Agency officers testing rivers.

Sir James downplayed concerns about pollution while at the helm of the Environment Agency. In 2023, he told a water industry conference that the debate around sewage pollution should be held “on the basis of facts not assertions”, adding: “There are some wild assertions, myths, and outright untruths flying around.”

The former Environment Agency chief also faced accusations of gagging staff. In an email to employees, Sir James said staff risked being sacked if they criticised or discredited the Environment Agency. Staff were even warned about commenting on the Environment Agency negatively inside work.

Southern Water was fined £90m during Sir James’s tenure after admitting to thousands of pollution incidents. It became clear that the Environment Agency had failed to identify that Southern Water was misreporting sewage discharges.

Carly Hardwidge, who set up the petition, said: “Revoking the knighthood from Sir James would send a powerful message. This action would restore the public’s faith in our honours system and reinforce the laudable aims of environmental accountability.”

Feargal Sharkey, the veteran water campaigner, backed the petition, saying: “I’d have to support the idea that he needs his knighthood removed because I can think of no reason why he should have ever earned it nor be allowed to retain it.”

‘Rewarding failure’

Since leaving the Environment Agency, Sir James has gone on to be a non-executive director at Welsh Water as well as a trustee of the Canal and Rivers Trust.

Earlier this week Welsh Water was hit with a £44.7m penalty following breaches in the supplier’s sewage network.

Henry Tuffnell, a Labour MP in Wales who sits on the Environment Committee, said: “Welsh Water dramatically needs to improve their environmental performance, investment and relationship with customers.”

Mr Tuffnell added: “It is therefore very concerning that they have rewarded failure in hiring Sir James Bevan, who has such a poor performance record from the EA.”

One of the key criticisms of the utilities industry is that weak executives move into the non-exec roles where they are ill-suited to challenging poor performance.

Alistair Carmichael, the head of the Environment Committee, said: “The problems of the water industry are ultimately problems of culture within the industry. Many of the problems come from poor corporate governance where under-performing executives are not properly challenged by regulators and their own non-exec directors.”

He added: “A revolving door where people can move easily from being poachers to gamekeepers serves only to make that problem worse. We will know if the Government is serious about changing the culture of the industry if, when they set up their new regulator, they take steps to lock that revolving door.”

A spokesman for Welsh Water said: “We appointed Sir James Bevan as a non-executive director in February 2025 following a rigorous search and appointment process.

“The Channel 4 programme is a television drama based on historical events relating to regulatory matters in England. Some dialogue, characters and scenes have been ‘recreated’ for the purposes of dramatisation. The portrayal of Sir James Bevan does not reflect the experience of the board since his appointment.”

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Oil crisis could hand Reeves almost £3bn tax windfall
data.description:
Rachel Reeves will bank almost £3bn in extra tax revenues if the war in the Middle East leads to a sustained increase in oil and gas prices, new analysis shows.

Soaring energy costs are likely to increase the corporation tax paid by North Sea firms by £1.5bn alone, according to Granville Park Partners.

Using the Office for Budget Responsibility’s (OBR’s) own rules of thumb, the consultancy added that a windfall tax on energy companies’ profits would boost the Treasury’s coffers by a further £1.3bn if current oil and gas prices are sustained.

It comes as pressure mounts on the Chancellor to help struggling households with their energy bills.

Cornwall Insight predicted last week that energy regulator Ofgem’s price cap was on track to rise by more than 10pc in July to £1,827 from £1,641 in April.

On Monday, Sir Keir Starmer unveiled a £53m emergency fund for “vulnerable” families using heating oil. Many rural households are not protected by the price cap because they are not connected to the main gas grid.

‘Completely unacceptable’

The Prime Minister warned that Labour would not tolerate price gouging, adding that he had issued a “legal direction” to energy companies to pass on savings from government policies.

Sir Keir said: “I simply will not allow companies to make huge profits from the hardship of working people.

“That kind of conduct is completely unacceptable, so if the companies have broken the law, there will be legal action.”

Granville Park Partners, led by Adam Smith, Jeremy Hunt’s former chief of staff, said oil prices – currently trading above $100 a barrel – were much higher than the $63 assumed by the OBR for 2026.

“Relative to the March 2026 forecast, higher energy prices could increase receipts by around £2.7bn per year for every year prices remain elevated at today’s levels,” he said.

However, Mr Smith added that higher oil prices would also negatively hit the public finances.

Soaring energy costs are set to pile pressure on Ms Reeves to increase public spending budgets which are set in cash terms, and potentially could force higher spending on welfare payments, which are usually uprated each year in line with the cost of living.

Factory bosses have called for Ed Miliband to approve extraction at Rosebank, Britain’s biggest undeveloped oil field, to prevent an energy crisis.

The Energy Secretary is also facing demands from unions and the manufacturing sector to approve production at Jackdaw, an undeveloped gas field in British waters that could start supplying the country as soon as this summer.

‘Opportunity foregone’

Martin Beck, the chief economist at WPI Strategy, said the potential boost to the public purse and economy from North Sea oil and gas was huge.

He said: “The fact that oil and gas could still generate that kind of revenue, even with North Sea output languishing at multi-decade lows, says a great deal about the scale of the opportunity the UK has forgone.

“By blocking new North Sea licences, banning fracking and generally making life harder for domestic producers, policymakers have denied the country one of its clearest defences against an energy price shock.”

Rosebank, which could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil, is the North Sea’s largest undeveloped site. Jackdaw is mainly gas and could provide between 6pc and 7pc of UK production.

Mr Beck said: “More UK oil and gas production would not have stopped global prices from rising. But it would have meant more jobs, more investment and, crucially, far higher tax receipts when the Treasury needed them most.

“That would have given the Government more firepower to help households and businesses, strengthened the public finances and kept borrowing costs lower than otherwise.

The OBR currently expects the Treasury to raise £4.1bn in taxes from the oil and gas sector this financial year.

data.title: Middle classes shouldn’t get help with bills, says Labour’s favourite think tank
data.description:
Rachel Reeves must deny middle-class households support with surging energy prices to help protect the public finances, a think tank with close links to Labour has said.

The Resolution Foundation, which has considerable influence in Labour circles, said the Chancellor should help only low-income households rather than repeating a 2022 programme introduced by Liz Truss, which delivered handouts to all.

It criticised the “universal blank cheque approach” of Ms Truss’s Energy Price Guarantee, introduced during the crisis triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

Instead, Ms Reeves should only help “lower-income families” if measures are introduced to counter rising prices, which have been made worse by the US-Iran war.

Ruth Curtice, the think tank’s chief executive, said a discounted “social tariff” could save poor households £310, rising to £520 for those with high energy needs, with a cost to the taxpayer of £3.8bn a year.

Offering “wasteful blanket support to households regardless of incomes” would be more expensive, she added.

Any support that excludes millions of middle-class households is likely to prove controversial.

Energy prices have emerged as a lightning rod for voter discontent.

Reform on Tuesday promised to pay the energy bills of an entire street as part of a lottery-style competition, highlighting growing concerns over soaring prices.

The Resolution Foundation’s warning is the latest in a series of calls on the Chancellor to exclude middle-class families from support with energy costs.

Industry group Energy UK has called for the Government to “immediately step up efforts” to prepare support for those who would struggle most when energy costs rise. The trade body urged the Government to target support only at low and lower-middle-income customers.

Doing so would cost the Treasury £12.5bn, compared to the £35bn universal support brought in after the Ukraine war shock in 2022, it said.

KPMG estimates that keeping the price cap at April’s level in the summer, instead of rising with higher wholesale energy prices as expected, would cost the Exchequer £5bn for just three months.

Campaigners at the End Fuel Poverty Coalition have also written to the Government demanding that preparations be made to keep bills down when the price cap is reset in the summer.

The group also called for a larger Warm Homes Discount for those on low incomes and relief for those behind on their bills.

Ms Reeves has indicated she is considering ways to offer targeted support, cutting out those on middle and higher incomes.

“We are working on different ways to protect people, including more targeted support,” she told MPs last week, adding that more data on household incomes is available now than was the case four years ago in the previous energy crisis.

Oil is trading at just over $100 (£75) per barrel, up from below $70 before the war, and drivers are already feeling the hit. Petrol prices have already risen by 10p per litre, according to the RAC, with diesel up 20p.

In her Mais Lecture on Tuesday, the Chancellor said she is focused on ending the war in Iran and preventing businesses from profiteering while it continues.

“The single best way to protect families and businesses from rising energy prices is a swift resolution to the conflict in the Middle East,” she said.

The Government is also in a worse position now than it was before the energy crisis of 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine, with higher debts and mounting interest payments.

That situation is getting worse as borrowing costs have jumped in recent weeks, rising faster in the UK than in other G7 nations.

Nicolas Crittenden, from the National Institute of Economic and Social Research, said the increase threatens to wipe out the Chancellor’s financial headroom toward her goal of paying for day-to-day spending with taxes rather than borrowing.

“If yields remain this high for the foreseeable future, it could pose a challenge to the Government’s current plans to remain within its fiscal rules,” he said.

“The UK bond market remains highly sensitive to changes in policy direction and external shocks. Recent events are a sobering reminder that the UK Government must find a credible path to reduce debt and make the economy more resilient to external shocks.”

data.title: Rothschild banking dynasty cashes out of The Economist
data.description:
Lynn Forester de Rothschild, a British philanthropist, has struck a deal to sell her stake in The Economist to a little-known Canadian billionaire.

Lady de Rothschild and her banking dynasty have agreed to sell a 26.9pc holding in the magazine to Stephen Smith, a financial services tycoon whose net worth is estimated at $7bn (£5.2bn) by Forbes.

The value of the stake was not disclosed, but was previously reported to be worth as much as £400m.

The deal marks the biggest shake-up of The Economist’s ownership since 2015, when British education publisher Pearson sold the majority of its 50pc stake alongside its sale of The Financial Times.

Those shares were bought up by Exor, the holding company of Italy’s carmaking dynasty, the Agnellis, which is now the largest shareholder in the magazine with a 43pc stake.

Lady de Rothschild’s stake in The Economist was held through EL Rothschild, a holding company she established in 2003 with her late third husband, Sir Evelyn de Rothschild, whom she married in 2000.

The American-born businesswoman hired advisers at Lazard last year to explore a sale after reportedly pushing for higher returns. The decision to cash in her 20pc holding prompted her family to follow suit.

Lady de Rothschild, who made her fortune in the telecoms industry after founding US broadband firm FirstMark Communications in 1995, is now in a relationship with Lord Saatchi, the 79-year-old advertising guru and former Conservative Party chairman.

The divestment comes at a critical time for The Economist, which was founded in 1843 by James Wilson, a Scottish hat manufacturer, to promote free trade.

Its philosophy is one of editorial independence, and its constitution bans any individual or organisation from owning a majority share, while the editor is appointed by trustees and it does not use bylines.

The weekly title is based in London but has expanded internationally and now employs 1,540 people in 26 countries.

The Economist reported revenues of £369m and a profit of £48m in the year to the end of March 2025. Its subscriber numbers increased by 3pc to 1.25 million.

But the title is now facing searching questions about its future in the digital age. The magazine has begun to shed its traditional anonymous style in favour of personality-led journalism by mounting a major push into video content.

Its political stance as a socially liberal, pro-free-trade title has also come under pressure in an era of growing polarisation.

Zanny Minton Beddoes, who has been editor of The Economist since 2015, extended her tenure by a further two years in October, breaking the tradition of editors holding a 10-year term.

Patrick Foulis, the magazine’s foreign editor, who was widely viewed as heir apparent, stepped down abruptly in the wake of the announcement.

Mr Smith has bought the near 27pc stake through Smith Financial Group, the family holding company he runs.

The 74-year-old tycoon, who grew up in Ottawa but was educated at the London School of Economics, has remained largely out of the spotlight in his home country.

He began his career flipping houses in Toronto in the 1980s before rising interest rates and a string of bad deals forced him into bankruptcy.

In 1988, Mr Smith and his partner started First National Financial Corporation, a mortgage lender that has grown to a market value of more than $2bn.

Mr Smith, who also holds major stakes in Canada Guaranty Mortgage Insurance Company and Equitable Bank, is known for his philanthropy.

He donated more than $100m to Queen’s University Canada, while he is chairman of Historica Canada, a charity dedicated to promoting the country’s history and citizenship.

A spokesman for Mr Smith told Bloomberg: “This investment reflects Mr Smith’s full support for The Economist’s long‑standing tradition of rigorous editorial independence and will see The Economist’s strategy and operations continue unaffected.”

A spokesman for The Economist said: “We can confirm that Stephen Smith, together with his family holding company, Smith Financial, has entered into an agreement to acquire a 26.9pc minority stake in The Economist Group from existing shareholders, Lady Lynn Forester de Rothschild, her family and her family foundation, subject to certain closing conditions.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest because of his stake in a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is still listed as a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, co-founded the firm and is a minority shareholder.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, such as transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concern owing to his role at CCW, a government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the environment, food and rural affairs committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

After publication, Mr Wilson claimed that he had not undertaken any client advisory work for the firm for more than two years, and none at all since his appointment as CCW chairman. He said the website was outdated.

He also stressed that before that, he did not advise any clients in the public utilities sector. He denies that his stake in Political Insight gives rise to any conflict of interest regarding his role at the CCW.

The row will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 320-metre car journey.

As well as owning a stake in Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Iran’s attack on Qatar gas citadel blows up Britain’s energy security
data.description:
Soaring prices, fuel shortages and possibly even blackouts: Iran’s latest attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure suggest the impacts of the conflict will be with us for many months to come.

Missiles launched from Iran rained down on the Ras Laffan plant in Qatar overnight, a facility so large that it produces around a fifth of the global supply of liquefied natural gas (LNG).

Many see the attack as a turning point in the war, signalling it will no longer be over in weeks, as Donald Trump promised, but will instead take many months.

It also marks a significant escalation in the conduct of the conflict: until now, Iran has avoided attacking energy infrastructure directly.

Qatar was the biggest supplier of LNG to the UK until 2022, accounting for 40pc of all imports at its peak in 2011. LNG is a key fuel for the UK, which still relies on gas for 35pc of its energy.

Imports have declined dramatically in recent years as the UK has turned to alternative supply from the US, with Qatari LNG now only accounting for around 2pc of imports.

Yet that does not mean Britain will be insulated from the crisis. Gas is a commodity traded globally and a shock in one geography affects the whole market.

European gas prices soared from €55 per megawatt-hour (MWh) to €72/MWh when that market opened on Thursday, a huge jump even before the scale of the damage was known. Oil has surged too – up around 8pc to $116 a barrel. Just a few weeks ago it was trading at $65.

Crisis looms

Qatar Energy, the owner of the Ras Laffan site, said the plant has suffered “extensive” damage, suggesting it could be disabled for months.

Analysts say the impact could be disastrous. Natasha Fielding, who leads on gas and LNG for Argus Media, a commodities specialist, says long-term damage could see prices hit 2022 energy crisis levels.

“If there is long-term damage to the LNG facility – which we still do not know – then it would not be surprising to see prices at the European [benchmark] push significantly higher,” she says.

“They could get closer to the eye-watering heights in the 2022 energy crisis.”

Seb Kennedy, founder of Energy Flux, a gas and LNG analytics platform, takes an even more pessimistic view.

“Rapid escalatory attacks and destruction of critical energy infrastructure are on a scale that defies historical comparison,” he says.

“This spiralling conflict is sowing the seeds of an energy crisis that will dwarf the events of 2021-22.”

What would that mean for the UK? In 2022, the typical annual household energy bill soared from £1,277 to £3,549 in just a few months.

Households never paid that price because the government stepped in to cap average bills at £2,500, but the cost of that was paid through taxes – an impact that has helped undermine the UK’s economy ever since.

We could be heading that way again. In fact, the current crisis means we could see shortages and even blackouts, according to Ashley Kelty of Panmure Liberum investment bank.

“There is a huge risk of shortages – north-west Europe could run out of gas as storage is very limited and competition from Asia means LNG cargoes will be diverted at the least. Load-shedding [where heavy industry is asked to shut down] and even blackouts in the summer no longer look fanciful.”

All forms of energy are set to rise sharply in price – power, gas, petrol, diesel and jet fuel – and the effect on the UK is set to be profound.

Greg Newman, founder and chief executive of Onyx Capital, a major London-based trader in oil and energy derivatives, predicts forecourt prices will hit £2 a litre for diesel as early as next month.

“What is concerning for the UK is that European oil prices, which were lagging behind the Middle East and Asia, are now aggressively catching up – and there is a long way to go for prices just to align with the rest of the world,” he says.

The price of diesel has already risen by more than 20p a litre in the UK to 162.66p since the conflict began, according to the RAC, while petrol has risen by almost 10p a litre to 142.62p.

“We are very confident the diesel price will surpass £2 a litre in the next month,” says Mr Newman.

Iran triggers chaos

What is Iran’s strategy? And why is it attacking facilities owned by neighbours who have taken no part in the US-Israeli strikes?

It’s worth remembering that Iran did not start this war – and nor did it start the attacks on energy infrastructure.

In the first week of Operation Epic Fury, the US and Israel focused on air defences, missile silos and leadership.

Energy infrastructure was largely spared to avoid a global energy crisis, a clear risk right from the start.

All that changed on March 7 when the Israeli Air Force struck multiple fuel storage facilities and oil depots in Tehran’s suburbs.

This week Israel shifted its attacks to target facilities that produce oil and gas, rather than just storing it.

That included South Pars, the world’s largest natural gas field, and the Assaluyeh refinery complex and gas processing plants. That knocked out 20pc of Iran’s gas processing capacity and caused widespread blackouts across Tehran and central Iran. Some 90pc of Iran’s electricity comes from gas-powered plants.

With Israel and the US well protected against retaliation, Iran’s best and perhaps only hope of putting pressure on its enemies lies in disrupting global energy supplies.

It is targeting facilities across the region but there are five key sites it really wants to damage, according to Aditya Saraswat, senior vice-president at global energy analysts Rystad Energy.

Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG plant is top of the list given its importance in supplying gas globally. Iran’s strike has knocked out the LNG “trains”, or processing lines, supplying Europe, Japan, South Korea and China under long-term contracts.

Similarly, Saudi Arabia’s advanced Samref refinery has faced a series of attacks, the latest on Thursday, so far with little apparent damage. It processes 402,000 barrels of oil a day into petrol, diesel, jet fuel and many more products.

“This is truly the worst case scenario for energy and fuel markets the world over. As usual, the poorest in society stand first in line to bear the brunt of the economic impacts,” says Mr Kennedy.

“Export controls and rationing are already under way in numerous Asian economies. But the scale of supply loss exceeds the ability of governments to manage demand destruction in an orderly fashion.”

The Middle East produces far more than just gas and oil, however. Iran is also targeting Saudi Arabia’s Al Jubail isocyanate production facility, which makes polyurethanes needed for a wide range of plastics used in industries ranging from construction to automotive.

Also under fire is Qatar’s Mesaieed Petrochemical Complex, 25 miles south of Doha, which turns gas and oil into vital chemicals such as polyethylene, the basis for thousands of other plastic products.

And Al Hosn sulphur plant in the United Arab Emirates is a target too. The plant lies above the Shah gas field whose “sour” gas is a key source of sulphur used in the global production of fertilisers and sulphuric acid – perhaps the world’s most widely used chemical.

Mr Saraswat says: “The breadth of what is at risk here in fuels, chemicals, LNG and fertiliser inputs is what makes this moment qualitatively different from previous episodes of Gulf tension.”

Mr Kennedy says: “Even if military warfare ends immediately, the energy situation is going to get a lot worse before it gets better.”

The mounting pressure on energy is increasing pressure on the UK Government to step in and help struggling households and businesses. Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, has said “nothing is off the table”. But with public finances still bearing the scars of the last round of support in 2022, there is a practical limit to just how far ministers can go.

Whatever happens, the cost of energy is set to be a key issue in the upcoming local and regional elections, including the Scottish and Welsh parliamentary elections, seen as vital in deciding whether Sir Keir Starmer still has a future as PM.

Our energy security has been shattered. And while it is not his fault, it may be Sir Keir who pays the price.

data.title: ‘Everyone is getting beaten with a stick’: Tradesmen turn their backs on Britain
data.description:
Vince Towns never planned to down his tools and leave Britain at the age of 51.

Having worked as a tradesman for 24 years, he hoped he would continue going for at least another decade so that he could pass on everything he knew to his two teenage sons.

But tax rises, soaring costs and suffocating bureaucracy have pushed him towards the exit long before his time.

“It’s just not worth me doing it,” he says. “Why should I bother? More and more is being taken away from me. I’ve lost so much heart in it now that I don’t want to put my body through it any more.”

Towns is making arrangements to move from Nottingham to Spain as soon as his wife obtains her Irish passport. When he gets there, he is considering continuing his work in construction.

And he thinks other tradesmen will soon follow him. “Everyone is getting beaten with a stick. People are earning just enough to live and you’ve seen how many businesses are going under – 2026 will be a big year for builders dropping out,” he says.

As Labour pursues its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, it has a real problem on its hands. To achieve its housebuilding dream, it needs an additional 300,000 construction workers in a range of trades from carpenters and bricklayers to plumbers, plasterers and roofers.

But not only are older workers throwing in the towel, younger workers are also seeking opportunities abroad, put off by punitive government policies, particularly on small businesses.

Since the start of Covid, Britain’s construction workforce has shrunk by 280,000 to just above two million, its lowest level in almost 25 years.

Meanwhile, other countries’ workforces have grown. In the same period, Australia added 183,000 tradesmen and builders, equivalent to a 15pc jump.

As the UK struggles to hold on to its workers, Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s decision to raise taxes for businesses threatens to accelerate the exodus and leave Britain trapped in a construction crisis for generations to come.

‘You get a better life back in Romania’

The foundations of Britain’s current construction industry crisis were laid around more than a decade ago as the world was still recovering from the global financial crisis.

Housebuilding starts had collapsed from around 180,000 before the crash to less than 80,000 by 2009, wiping out companies and triggering hundreds of thousands of job losses. As activity began to pick up again, however, construction firms increasingly turned to skilled migrant workers rather than invest in training up apprentices.

Professor Iain Begg, an expert on Eastern European economics at LSE, says the trend was fuelled by Poland’s entry into the EU in 2004, followed by Romania and Bulgaria in 2007, which opened the door to an influx of cheap labour.

“Employers could suddenly say, ‘I can hire a Pole rather than train a Brit’,” he says.

By 2016, non-UK-born workers made up 14.2pc of the entire sector, having comprised just 7pc in 2007. In London, this figure was as high as 40pc, according to the Office for National Statistics.

However, following the Brexit vote in 2016 and the shock of the pandemic, the inflow of migrants that had defined the previous decade went into reverse. The number of Poles in the UK fell from a peak of just over a million in 2017 to 698,000 four years later.

For many Eastern Europeans working in Britain, the twin effects of Brexit and Covid became an “inflection point”, says Prof Begg. “It motivated quite a lot of Poles in the UK to say, ‘okay, I’ve been here, done that, now is the time for me to move on and do something new’.”

Because the construction industry heavily relied on skilled migrants, it was disproportionately hit by the exodus and exposed a chronic lack of training for UK workers.

Yet even as the dust has settled on Brexit and the pandemic, foreign-born workers are continuing to up sticks as taxes and rising costs take the shine off higher salaries.

Florin Napa, a 27-year-old carpenter who moved to the UK from Romania in 2018, says he is planning on moving back after seeing a growing proportion of his income go to the taxman.

“Before it was fine because I could save money,” says Napa, who shared an apartment with several other foreign workers during his first few years in the UK. “When I arrived I wanted to buy a nice car and do something with my life.

“But I feel like now [as a labourer] if you don’t push yourself, work weekends and longer hours, longer days, it’s very hard to save money. Because the more you earn the more you pay [in tax].”

At the same time, wages and living standards in eastern European and Balkan countries have improved since they joined the EU. In 1990, for example, the GDP per capita in the UK was three times more than Romania; today the gap has closed to just 1.2.

This has convinced many like Napa that they will be better off back home. “You can now earn good money back in my country doing what I’m doing,” he says. “Carpenters can earn €4,000 a month, they have their own house, they don’t pay a mortgage. You’ve got a better life.”

‘Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates’

Much of the blame falls on governments that have for years squeezed small builders and businesses. But recent Labour policies affecting the cost of hiring staff have made conditions particularly tough.

Since coming into office, Reeves has raised both employer National Insurance and the minimum wage. This includes a jump in apprentice wages from £6.40 in 2024 to £8 from April this year.

At the same time rising material costs and shrinking pipelines of work as projects get held up in planning purgatory are squeezing profits to breaking point.

Rico Wojtulewicz, of the National Federation of Builders, says as a result it has become increasingly difficult for small construction businesses to recruit and train young workers.

“The Government sets the environment for business to succeed or fail,” he says. “If the industry is continually seen as a cash cow, and the Government thinks it can keep piling on costs or bureaucracy or tax, then those people are going to struggle to run their businesses.”

While big housing developers can typically ride out such pressures, small businesses, which provide jobs for 80pc of UK apprentices, are buckling under the strain.

There are fewer jobs for tradesmen: in the construction industry vacancies have fallen fallen 32pc in the last three months to 28,000, the largest percentage decline across all sectors.

When those entering a trade cannot see a secure career path for themselves, Wojtulewicz says it is inevitable they will either move abroad or leave the profession altogether. “Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates,” he says. “You can train a million people to do the jobs but if there aren’t a million career opportunities, then you’re wasting people’s time.”

‘You’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear’

Faced with diminishing career prospects, many young British labourers have moved to Australia, which has sought to tackle its own skills shortages by offering a A$10,000 visa grant scheme for UK tradesmen.

Between 2022 and 2024, the number of Australian visas issued to British carpenters and joiners more than doubled from 66 to 135, while the number of civil engineering professionals jumped from 72 to 179.

Charles Esterling-Cooper, who runs a relocation agency in Perth, says generous salaries, nice weather and a perception around better opportunities are helping to attract British labourers. “Skilled tradespeople often find jobs quickly, sometimes even before they get here,” he says.

“A lot of the tradespeople I talk to are moving with young families – and they’re coming for the long term.” He adds that only one of his clients has moved back to the UK in the last few years.

At the opposite end of the career ladder, the Making Tax Digital scheme is convincing some older workers to put down their tools early.

Under the new rules, self-employed workers with a gross income of £50,000 will be required to file quarterly tax reports using HMRC-compatible software. Currently they only need to submit one self-assessment form every tax year.

More than a third of the construction workforce is over the age of 50, meaning the sector is already facing a retirement cliff edge. Towns, who is preparing for his move to Spain, says it will pile on administrative costs and push others like him to quit the industry.

“To them, it’s a major hindrance. So you’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear and move into different things.”

And for experienced builders who do stay, Towns says there is little incentive to grow their businesses because of “nonsensical” tax thresholds, such as when VAT is triggered at a revenue of £90,000.

“There’s no point in going over that,” he says. “If you’re inefficient then the costs are going to outstrip any profit that you’re going to make. They’ve taken away the incentive to work.”

‘Construction underpins so much success in the country’

The consequences of Britain’s depleted construction industry workforce extend far beyond falling short of bold housebuilding pledges.

Skilled tradesmen are crucial for everything from kitchen extensions to vast public infrastructure projects. Without them, it creates an insurmountable hurdle that can have a damaging knock-on effect for other parts of the economy.

“Construction underpins so much success in the country, particularly growth,” says Wojtulewicz. “It’s not just 1.5 million homes, it’s all the stuff alongside it: the roads, the rail, the commercial, the retail, the amenities – all this stuff actually requires skilled labour.”

If the demand for such work remains, yet the workforce diminishes, it can also present an opportunity for less competent labourers to pick up jobs, warns Jeremy Gray, of trade association the Federation of Master Builders.

“Because you don’t have to have any minimum competence to be a builder, there are a lot of rogues out stealing business,” he says. “We’re getting more reports about a lot of remediation.”

The FMB estimates that over the last five years alone, homeowners have lost more than £14bn to unreliable builders.

Like Wojtulewicz, Gray also believes the country is being held back not just by a lack of new apprentices coming through but a distinct lack of opportunities for those that do.

The long-awaited Planning and Infrastructure Act, which came into law in December, may offer some reprieve for the construction industry.

For builders like Towns, however, it is far too late. After a quarter of a century of hard graft, he’s packing up his toolbox for the last time – with a lingering feeling of regret about how his career has ended.

“If I’d have known how things were going to change, I wouldn’t have done all that work and put my body through the stress,” he says.

“Your natural instinct is to work hard, have a family, educate them, save a bit for them and help them along. That’s the natural progression and it feels like it’s going backwards.”

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “This Government inherited a dire shortage of construction workers, and we are determined to deliver more opportunities for young people as we work to boost construction skills.

“We have committed £625m to recruit an additional 60,000 construction workers and £1.5bn to get hundreds of thousands of young people earning or learning, including in construction.

“We’re leaving no stone unturned to build 1.5 million homes and we’re already seeing green shoots of recovery with an 18pc increase in housing starts last year.”
